title: User Rewards Stop Accruing After Checkpoint Actions Due to Off-by-One
description: In _writeCheckpoint(), the code reads checkpoints[account][_nCheckPoints] instead of checkpoints[account][_nCheckPoints - 1] when fetching the previous checkpoint's voted status. This reads an uninitialized storage slot which defaults to false. As a result, any action that triggers a new checkpoint (deposit, withdraw, getReward) resets the voted flag to false, halting reward accrual until the user votes again.
impact: Users who have voted and then perform any balance-changing action (deposit more LP, withdraw, or claim rewards) silently lose their voting status. Their rewards stop accruing until they vote again, even though they never intended to stop voting.
recommendation: Fix the index: read checkpoints[account][_nCheckPoints - 1].voted instead of checkpoints[account][_nCheckPoints].voted.
educational_note: Off-by-one errors in checkpoint systems are a classic vulnerability class. The Uniswap V2 build module doesn't use checkpoints, but the concept of reading the 'previous' value vs 'current' value in storage arrays is fundamental. The lesson: when code references array[index], always verify whether it should be array[index] or array[index - 1]. Test with concrete values: if _nCheckPoints is 2, should we read slot 2 (uninitialized) or slot 1 (last written)?
build_module_ref: Section 3 (Pair: State and Initialization) covers storage patterns. This finding extends that knowledge to checkpoint-based accounting.

title: Bribe Rewards Permanently Locked If Deposited During First Epoch
description: Voter.distribute() only calls deliverBribes() when VELO emission claimable rewards exceed zero. VELO emissions start in epoch 2+. Therefore, bribes deposited during epoch 0 have no trigger to be delivered to the Gauge, and there is no alternative claim mechanism.
impact: All bribe rewards deposited during the first epoch are permanently locked in the Bribe contract. There is no admin function or alternative path to recover them.
recommendation: Allow deliverBribes() to be called independently of VELO emission thresholds, or add a manual trigger for first-epoch bribes.
educational_note: This is a state machine initialization bug. The system assumes a precondition (VELO emissions > 0) that is not true during the first epoch. The lesson: always trace the complete lifecycle from deployment/initialization through steady state. What happens in epoch 0? What happens if a function's trigger condition is never met? Edge cases at system boundaries (first user, first epoch, empty state) are where many real-world bugs hide.
build_module_ref: Section 5 (Pair: Adding Liquidity) covers the first-depositor edge case with MINIMUM_LIQUIDITY. Same principle: system initialization requires special handling.

title: Voting Overwrites Checkpoint, Enabling Vote-Before-Claim Reward Exploit
description: The checkpoint's voted status is overwritten in-place instead of creating a new checkpoint entry when votes change. A user can vote immediately before claiming rewards in the same transaction, getting full-period rewards without sustained voting commitment.
impact: Users game the reward system by voting just-in-time before claiming, then resetting votes immediately after. This dilutes rewards for honest long-term voters.
recommendation: Create a new checkpoint on vote state changes instead of overwriting the existing one, or use a time-weighted voting requirement.
educational_note: This is a timing/ordering exploit. The system assumes voting is a sustained commitment, but nothing enforces temporal ordering between vote and claim within a single transaction. The lesson: whenever a reward depends on a state (voted = true), check if that state can be set and consumed in the same transaction. If yes, the time-weighting assumption is broken.
build_module_ref: Section 7 (Pair: The Swap) covers the K invariant check that happens after state changes. Same principle: verify the invariant AFTER all state changes, not before.

title: Gauge Setup Can Be Front-Run Due to Permissionless setGauge()
description: Bribe.setGauge() is publicly callable without access control. Between Bribe deployment and Gauge deployment, an attacker can front-run to set an incorrect gauge address.
impact: Requires full redeployment of Bribe contracts. Griefing attack that wastes gas and disrupts protocol setup.
recommendation: Add access control: only the factory or deployer should be able to call setGauge().
educational_note: Missing access control on initialization functions is one of the most common vulnerability classes. In Uniswap V2, the Pair's initialize() function checks msg.sender == factory. ElasticSwap similarly restricts initialization. Velodrome's Bribe forgot this basic pattern. The lesson: every function that sets critical state during initialization needs access control.
build_module_ref: Section 8 (The Factory) covers how createPair calls initialize() with factory-only access. This finding is the direct consequence of omitting that pattern.

title: Malicious Population of Rewards Array via Permissionless notifyRewardAmount
description: notifyRewardAmount() is permissionless. Anyone can add arbitrary ERC20 tokens as reward tokens with dust amounts. Once MAX_REWARD_TOKENS is reached, legitimate reward tokens cannot be added.
impact: Denial of service on the reward system. An attacker fills the reward token array with garbage tokens, blocking legitimate bribe/reward deposits.
recommendation: Add a whitelist or minimum deposit requirement for new reward tokens, or restrict notifyRewardAmount to approved callers.
educational_note: Permissionless functions that modify bounded arrays are a classic DoS vector. The lesson: whenever you see an array with a max length AND a permissionless function that appends to it, the DoS is immediate. Ask: who can call this? What does it cost them? Can they fill it with garbage?
build_module_ref: Section 8 (The Factory) covers the allPairs array, which grows unbounded but is append-only by the factory. Velodrome's reward array is bounded but fillable by anyone.

title: Fee-on-Transfer Tokens Cause Permanent Reward Lock in Bribe
description: Bribe records the declared transfer amount without verifying the actual received balance. For fee-on-transfer tokens, the contract holds fewer tokens than its accounting expects. When distribution attempts to send the recorded amount, it reverts due to insufficient balance.
impact: Fee-on-transfer bribe rewards are permanently locked. Distribution reverts, blocking claims for that token.
recommendation: Measure balance before and after transfer to determine actual received amount, or explicitly disallow fee-on-transfer tokens.
educational_note: The Uniswap V2 Router has dedicated 'SupportingFeeOnTransferTokens' variants that measure balance before/after. The build module Section 15 (Router: Fee-on-Transfer Support) covers exactly this pattern. Velodrome's Bribe contract fails to account for it. The lesson: any contract that receives tokens and later distributes them must handle the case where actual received != declared amount.
build_module_ref: Section 15 (Router: Fee-on-Transfer Support) covers the balance-before/after pattern for FOT tokens.

title: Temporary DoS via Malicious Reverting Reward Token
description: deliverBribes() and getReward() loop through all reward tokens. If any single token transfer reverts (malicious token or paused token), the entire function reverts. Combined with M-04 (permissionless reward token addition), an attacker can add a reverting token to block all reward operations.
impact: Complete DoS of reward distribution and claims. All legitimate rewards are blocked by a single malicious token until admin intervention.
recommendation: Use try/catch around individual token transfers in the loop, or implement a pull-based claim pattern where users claim per-token.
educational_note: This is a composability bug: M-04 (fill rewards with garbage) + M-08 (one revert blocks all) combine into a critical DoS chain. In Uniswap V2, the pair only deals with exactly 2 tokens, avoiding unbounded loops entirely. The lesson: loops over external calls are dangerous. Each iteration is an opportunity for revert, and one failure can cascade to block everything.
build_module_ref: Section 7 (Pair: The Swap) covers the two-token swap pattern. Velodrome's multi-token reward loop is the anti-pattern.

title: Rewards Not Updated Before Balance Change in withdrawToken()
description: withdrawToken() is publicly callable and directly modifies user balances without first calling _updateRewardForAllTokens(). The intended path is through withdraw() which updates rewards before changing balances, but withdrawToken() bypasses this.
impact: Users who call withdrawToken() directly lose accumulated rewards. The reward accounting becomes corrupted because it assumes balances are constant between updates.
recommendation: Make withdrawToken() internal or add the reward update call before the balance change.
educational_note: This is a Checks-Effects-Interactions violation, specifically the 'Effects' ordering. In Uniswap V2's burn(), _mintFee is called BEFORE the balance change to ensure fee accounting is correct before any state changes. The build module Section 6 explicitly teaches this ordering. Velodrome has the correct ordering in withdraw() but forgot to enforce it in the alternative entry point withdrawToken(). The lesson: when multiple functions modify the same state, ALL of them must maintain the same invariants.
build_module_ref: Section 6 (Pair: Removing Liquidity) covers the _mintFee-before-balance-change ordering requirement.

title: Reward Rate Griefing via Dust notifyRewardAmount Calls
description: Anyone can call notifyRewardAmount() with dust amounts during an active reward period. Each call resets the reward period duration and recalculates the rate with the remaining + new (dust) amount spread over a fresh period. This effectively dilutes the reward rate.
impact: Attacker can continuously extend reward periods and dilute rates at minimal cost (especially on L2 where gas is cheap). Stakers receive rewards much slower than intended.
recommendation: Restrict notifyRewardAmount to authorized callers, or add a minimum reward amount threshold.
educational_note: This is an economic griefing attack. The code is 'correct' in the sense that it does exactly what it says (add rewards, reset period). But the permissionless access + L2 cheap gas makes it economically viable to grief. The lesson: correct code can still be vulnerable if the economic assumptions (calling this costs meaningful gas) don't hold in all deployment contexts.

title: Bribes Permanently Locked When distribute() Skips deliverBribes()
description: distribute() only calls deliverBribes() when VELO claimable rewards exceed a minimum threshold. Bribes deposited independently are not delivered if the VELO threshold is not met in the same epoch.
impact: Bribe rewards become permanently stuck in the Bribe contract for any epoch where VELO distribution does not occur or is below threshold.
recommendation: Decouple bribe delivery from VELO distribution thresholds. Allow deliverBribes() to be called independently.
educational_note: This is a coupling bug. Two independent operations (VELO distribution and bribe delivery) are coupled through a shared trigger condition. When one operation's precondition fails, the other is collateral damage. The lesson: when function A triggers function B as a side effect, ask: can B's preconditions ever be met independently of A's? If yes, B needs its own trigger.

title: Unclaimed Bribe Rewards From Past Epochs Are Permanently Lost
description: Rewards are keyed by epoch with no alternate claim mechanism for missed epochs. If deliverReward() is not called for a particular epoch, those rewards are permanently irretrievable.
impact: Bribe rewards from any missed epoch are lost forever. No admin recovery function exists.
recommendation: Add a sweep function for unclaimed epoch rewards, or allow retroactive delivery across epochs.
educational_note: Time-boxed reward systems must handle the case where the claim window is missed. The lesson: for any time-dependent operation, ask: what happens if nobody calls this function during the expected window? Is the value recoverable?

title: Double Reward Delivery Due to Uncleared Epoch Rewards
description: deliverReward() does not zero out tokenRewardsPerEpoch[token][epochStart] after delivering rewards. The mapping retains the old value, allowing the same rewards to be delivered again if deliverReward() is called a second time for the same epoch.
impact: Double (or multiple) delivery of bribe rewards drains the Bribe contract. Accounting becomes corrupted, and later epochs may have insufficient funds.
educational_note: This is the classic 'state not reset after consumption' bug. In Uniswap V2, kLast is updated after _mintFee to prevent double-counting fee growth. The same principle applies here: after you consume a value (deliver rewards), zero it out. The lesson: every read-then-act pattern on stored values should be followed by a reset. If you read a balance, debit it. If you read a reward amount, zero it.
build_module_ref: Section 7 (Pair: The Swap) covers the K invariant update after swap completion. Same principle: consume then reset.

title: Incorrect rewardRate Recalculation Disadvantages Late Stakers
description: When extending rewards mid-period via notifyRewardAmount(), the recalculated rate improperly accounts for remaining time. The leftover from the previous period is added to the new amount but spread over a full new period, creating a rate that disadvantages participants who join after the recalculation.
impact: Late-joining stakers receive disproportionately fewer or zero rewards compared to what the total deposited amount should provide.
recommendation: Correctly calculate the remaining rewards from the current period and combine with the new deposit for the new period duration.
educational_note: Reward rate math with time-based distribution is notoriously tricky. The lesson: when a function recalculates a rate mid-stream, trace the math with concrete numbers. Pick a specific scenario (e.g., 100 tokens over 7 days, 3 days in, add 50 more). Calculate what each staker should receive. Then verify the code produces the same result.
